images.caption:
Figure 1:OmniGameArena at a glance. Twelve newly built UE5 games
span Solo (7), PvP (3), and Coop (2) regimes (top).
Heterogeneous agents (commercial VLMs, open-weight VLMs,
keyboard-mouse policies, and gamepad policies) connect to the
same real-time UE5 environment through documented adapters
(middle). Evaluation reports the cold-start leaderboard and the
Improvement Dynamics Curve (IDC) under multi-round reflection
(bottom).

Figure 2:Radar charts of the 12 OmniGameArena games across seven capability dimensions. The abbreviations are: VP = Visual Perception, SN = Spatial Navigation, RT = Reaction, MEM = Memory, PLN = Planning, ADV = Adversarial, and COOP = Cooperation. Each dimension is scored from 0 to 3.
Table 1:Summary of 12 Interactive Games.

Figure 3:Overview of the Improvement Dynamics Curve (IDC) harness. Theexperience acquisitionmodule (left) runsKKepisodes
under the current skill. Thereflectionmodule (center)
reads both the new trajectories and the persistent state
(notebook and prior skill), then runs four autonomous stages
(Explore, Diagnose, Validate, Distill) to produce a refined
skill. Thepersistentmodule (right) stores the
experience notebook, validated skills, and per-round score
curve, which seed the next round.
Table 3:Solo cold-start scores. Top 3 per column:red>>orange>>yellow.
Figure 4:PvP win rates of Player 1 (row) against Player 2 (column) per game over all pairings.

Table 4:Coop team scores on two cooperative games,
where two copies of the same model must coordinate to complete a shared task.

Table 5:Transfer of IDC best skill across the origin split and three unseen variants.
Each cell shows the gainVbest−V0V_{\text{best}}-V_{0}followed by the%\%-change relative toV0V_{0}.V0V_{0}is the no-skill baseline on that split;VbestV_{\text{best}}applies the skill that gave the highest mean score during the 10-round run.

Figure 5:IDC curves: per-round mean episode score across 10 reflection rounds for four agents on LastStand (left) and SharedFloor (right). Horizontal lines mark each agent’s R0 (no-skill PDQ baseline) for reference; deviations above the line indicate skill-driven improvement. Each round aggregates 5 episodes per agent.
Table 6:LCRT results on selected Solo games. Each cell reports mean with standard deviation as subscript overN=5N{=}5episodes, withΔ\Deltadenoting the change relative to the corresponding PDQ result.
Figure 6:PvP win rates of Player 1 (row) against Player 2 (column) on MidlineClash under latency control setting.

Table 7:LCRT results on the cooperative game SharedFloor. Each cell reports mean with standard deviation as subscript overN=5N{=}5episodes, withΔ\Deltadenoting the change relative to the corresponding PDQ result.

Table 8:SoloCraft action budget.Actions per episode and normalized score per action under PDQ vs. LCRT. Score per action is nearly unchanged, so each agent’s LCRT drop (Table˜6) is almost entirely its smaller action budget.

Table 9:SharedFloor action budget(actions and team score summed over both players; score normalized). The LCRT drop tracks the shrinking action budget: GPT-5.5, the slowest, completes by far the fewest actions, yet keeps the highest score per action under both protocols, so its loss is a budget effect rather than a per-action collapse.
Figure 7:Qualitative comparison onLast Standusing GPT-5.5.
Figure 8:Qualitative comparison onShared Floorusing Gemini-3.1-Pro.
Figure 9:Visualization results forcue_chase. Each row shows one model, with five sampled frames from the corresponding trajectory.
Figure 10:Visualization results forlast_stand. Each row shows one model, with five sampled frames from the corresponding trajectory.
Figure 11:Visualization results formonster_shoot. Each row shows one model, with five sampled frames from the corresponding trajectory.
Figure 12:Visualization results forobstacle_run_2d. Each row shows one model, with five sampled frames from the corresponding trajectory.
Figure 13:Visualization results forobstacle_run_3d. Each row shows one model, with five sampled frames from the corresponding trajectory.
Figure 14:Visualization results forscene_escape. Each row shows one model, with five sampled frames from the corresponding trajectory.
Figure 15:Visualization results forsolo_craft. Each row shows one model, with five sampled frames from the corresponding trajectory.
Figure 16:Visualization results forhandoff_run. Each row shows one cooperative model pair, with five sampled frames from the corresponding episode.
Figure 17:Visualization results forshared_floor. Each row shows one cooperative model pair, with five sampled frames from the corresponding episode.
Figure 18:Visualization results forcrystal_guard. Each row shows one representative PvP matchup, with five sampled frames from the corresponding match.
Figure 19:Visualization results formidline_clash. Each row shows one representative PvP matchup, with five sampled frames from the corresponding match.
Figure 20:Visualization results forsky_duel. Each row shows one representative PvP matchup, with five sampled frames from the corresponding match.
